feature","color":"02d7e1","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-08T22:47:25Z","updated_at":"2024-10-08T22:47:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Problem

There doesn't appear to be a good way to [get a gridspec from a figure](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/74550664/is-it-possible-to-get-gridspec-from-figure-before-adding-axes).  That link suggests using a since-removed private attribute.

### Proposed solution

Since [`Figure.add_gridspec()`](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/5a51fa219cffbd3744e0e9f161bd7655c08c7515/lib/matplotlib/figure.py#L1549) is described as "creating a `.GridSpec` that has this figure as a parent", have it also add the gridspec to the figure's list of children.  In other words, I interpreted the parent-child abstraction to mean the following assert should pass:

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig = plt.figure()
gs = fig.add_gridspec(2, 2)`
assert gs in fig.get_children()
```
But it does not.


Broadly, this is all under the troubleshooting intent of being able to move Axes from one figure to another.  There's probably a better way of factoring plot-generation code that would obviate this need, at least for me.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28955/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28955/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Part of #26404.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26624/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26624/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2402905951","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26624#issuecomment-2402905951","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26624","id":2402905951,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86POW9f","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-09T17:32:47Z","updated_at":"2024-10-09T17:32:47Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Hmm, this is curious.  `keepdims` defaults to `_NoValue`, not `None` or `False`, even though the default behavior is `False`.  Thus the static typing sees the failing test as overload 2, instead of overload 1. 

> If the default value is passed, then keepdims will not be passed through to the min method of sub-classes of [ndarray](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.html#numpy.ndarray), however any non-default value will be. If the sub-class’ method does not implement keepdims any exceptions will be raised.

In other words, `_NoValueType` exists in order to allow subclasses to break Liskov Substitution, which means might be a problem in and of itself.  It looks like `_NoValueType` [was first discussed](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/5683#issuecomment-80823178) as a sort of third option for an argument and has come up again in a few PRs and issues [1], [2], and [3].

The long and short of this is that you can _probably_ include `_NoValueType` in overload 1, e.g. `keep_dims: Literal[False] | _NoValueType`.  But it would also be good to check whether we really need to allow `_NoValue` as the default or we can just default to false.

[1]: #8991 
[2]: #13105 
[3]: #19173","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2402905951/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

> I couldn't understand how to make failing tests. 

Fail tests are written with arguments that fail type checking, with a comment afterwards for the expected type checking error (mostly either "no overload variant" or "argument has incompatible type" e.g. 

https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/d52816ae9f95781cc2a48cac0a8ca540e051f648/numpy/typing/tests/data/fail/fromnumeric.pyi#L126-L130

There's also reveal tests that verify the type that gets returned.  By comparison, pass/fail tests check arguments, reveal tests check return value.

> When calling spin mypy somehow it kept throwing me old errors even after deleting the test file.

Did you try running `$ git clean -xdf`?","commit_id":"d52816ae9f95781cc2a48cac0a8ca540e051f648","submitted_at":"2024-09-11T18:12:59Z","state":"commented","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26624#pullrequestreview-2293820603","pull_request_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/26624","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","_links":{"html":{"href":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26624#pullrequestreview-2293820603"},"pull_request":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/26624"}}},"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/26624","id":1905873776,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAA3dP85xmVNw","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26624","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26624.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26624.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26624","number":26624,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"TYP: type ma.min","user":{"login":"luxedo","id":14118472,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE0MTE4NDcy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/14118472?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/luxedo","html_url":"https://github.com/luxedo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/luxedo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/luxedo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/luxedo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/luxedo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/luxedo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/luxedo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/luxedo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/luxedo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/luxedo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"<!--         ----------------------------------------------------------------

> I couldn't understand how to make failing tests. 

Fail tests are written with arguments that fail type checking, with a comment afterwards for the expected type checking error (mostly either "no overload variant" or "argument has incompatible type" e.g. 

https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/d52816ae9f95781cc2a48cac0a8ca540e051f648/numpy/typing/tests/data/fail/fromnumeric.pyi#L126-L130

There's also reveal tests that verify the type that gets returned with `assert_type`.  My mental comparison is that pass/fail tests check arguments, reveal tests check return value.

> When calling spin mypy somehow it kept throwing me old errors even after deleting the test file.

result = np.ma.min(arr, axis=None, out=tgt)

```

Assume that `typing_extensions` is always available in the stubs","user":{"login":"jorenham","id":6208662,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYyMDg2NjI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6208662?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham","html_url":"https://github.com/jorenham","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Recently I found out that [`typing_extensions`](https://github.com/python/typing_extensions) is always available in `.pyi` stubs (and when `typing.TYPE_CHECKING`) -- even if it's not available at runtime.
This is because the [`typeshed`](https://github.com/python/typeshed/tree/main) stubs for the standard library includes the [`typing_extensions.pyi`](https://github.com/python/typeshed/blob/main/stdlib/typing_extensions.pyi) stubs, so that type-checkers consider it a standard library (even though it might not exist at runtime).

This allows for most of the conditional-import code in `*.pyi` to be simplified, and is what I've done in this PR.
Apart from it being cleaner, more DRY, more readable, and easier to maintain, it makes the type stubs less incorrect, since `if` statements are a runtime thing, not a typing thing. 
But even though type-checkers usually understood it in this particular case, it was bad practice to do so, and should be avoid if possible; hence this PR.

---

But the real great thing about this, is that we apparently are able to use all of those other cool new features from `typing_extensions` that aren't available in `typing` on Python 3.10.

The first example of this that comes to mind is [PEP 696](https://peps.python.org/pep-0696/); which we could use so that `complexfloating[N]` is an alias for `complexfloating[N, N]`.

Assume that `typing_extensions` is always available in the stubs","user":{"login":"jorenham","id":6208662,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYyMDg2NjI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6208662?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham","html_url":"https://github.com/jorenham","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Recently I found out that [`typing_extensions`](https://github.com/python/typing_extensions) is always available in `.pyi` stubs (and when `typing.TYPE_CHECKING`) -- even if it's not available at runtime.
This is because the [`typeshed`](https://github.com/python/typeshed/tree/main) stubs for the standard library includes the [`typing_extensions.pyi`](https://github.com/python/typeshed/blob/main/stdlib/typing_extensions.pyi) stubs, so that type-checkers consider it a standard library (even though it might not exist at runtime).

This allows for most of the conditional-import code in `*.pyi` to be simplified, and is what I've done in this PR.
Apart from it being cleaner, more DRY, more readable, and easier to maintain, it makes the type stubs less incorrect, since `if` statements are a runtime thing, not a typing thing. 
But even though type-checkers usually understood it in this particular case, it was bad practice to do so, and should be avoid if possible; hence this PR.

---

But the real great thing about this, is that we apparently are able to use all of those other cool new features from `typing_extensions` that aren't available in `typing` on Python 3.10.

The first example of this that comes to mind is [PEP 696](https://peps.python.org/pep-0696/); which we could use so that `complexfloating[N]` is an alias for `complexfloating[N, N]`.
It would also allow us to reduce the amount of overloads in case of parameters like `spam: T = ...` from 2 or 3 (in case it accepts both a positional or a keyword argument) to stubs","user":{"login":"jorenham","id":6208662,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYyMDg2NjI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6208662?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham","html_url":"https://github.com/jorenham","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Recently I found out that [`typing_extensions`](https://github.com/python/typing_extensions) is always available in `.pyi` stubs (and when `typing.TYPE_CHECKING`) -- even if it's not available at runtime.
This is because the [`typeshed`](https://github.com/python/typeshed/tree/main) stubs for the standard library includes the [`typing_extensions.pyi`](https://github.com/python/typeshed/blob/main/stdlib/typing_extensions.pyi) stubs, so that type-checkers consider it a standard library (even though it might not exist at runtime).

This allows for most of the conditional-import code in `*.pyi` to be simplified, and is what I've done in this PR.
Apart from it being cleaner, more DRY, more readable, and easier to maintain, it makes the type stubs less incorrect, since `if` statements are a runtime thing, not a typing thing. 
But even though type-checkers usually understood it in this particular case, it was bad practice to do so, and should be avoid if possible; hence this PR.

---

But the real great thing about this, is that we apparently are able to use all of those other cool new features from `typing_extensions` that aren't available in `typing` on Python 3.10.

The first example of this that comes to mind is [PEP 696](https://peps.python.org/pep-0696/); which we could use so that `complexfloating[N]` is an alias for `complexfloating[N, N]`.
It would also allow us to reduce the amount of overloads in case of parameters like `spam: T = ...` from 2 or 3 (in case it accepts both a positional or a keyword argument) to Running Mypy on some functions within `numpy.ma` lead to type checking errors due to missing annotations.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy

a = numpy.ma.array([1, 2, 3, 4], mask=[0, 0, 1, 0])
b = numpy.ma.masked_array([1, 2, 3, 4], mask=[0, 0, 1, 0])
```


### Error message:

```shell
$ mypy test.py 
test.py:3: error: Call to untyped function "array" in typed context  [no-untyped-call]
test.py:4: error: Call to untyped function "MaskedArray" in typed context  [no-untyped-call]
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

```
1.26.4
3.12.2 (main, Feb  6 2024, 20:19:44) [Clang 15.0.0 (clang-1500.1.0.2.5)]
```

### Context for the issue:

We rely on type checking to find bugs in our software that uses Numpy before it is executed. This enables higher code quality and reliability. False positive type errors cost time to track down and can distract from real issues.

Type annotations also help IDEs such as PyCharm and VS Code perform better intellisense during https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/16545; closes https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/16547.

Well folks, it's finally here: this pull requests makes the `np.ndarray` class generic w.r.t. its 
shape and dtype: `np.ndarray[~Shape, ~DType]`. The shape's bound is currently set
to `Any` (see "Non-Goals") while the dtype's bound is set to `np.dtype`.

~The "generification" of `np.ndarray` is accompanied by the addition of three types to the public 
API of `numpy.typing`, all of which are subscriptable during runtime (similar to the likes of 
`typing.Sequence[~T]`):~
* ~`NDArray`: A generic version of `np.ndarray[~Shape, ~DType]`.~
* ~`DType`: A generic version of `np.dtype[~Scalar]`.~
* ~`ArrayND`: A shortcut for `np.ndarray[Any, np.dtype[~Scalar]]`. I imagine that, at some point in 
  the future, we can expand this with the likes of `Array0D`, `Array1D`, `Array2D`, _etc._.~

Non-Goals
-----------
* EDIT: Providing runtime-subscriptable aliases for `ndarray`, similar to the likes of 
  `collections.abc.Sequence` versus `typing.Sequence`. This will be reserved for a future 
  PR as the exact details might warrant some further discussion.
* We currently lack the tools to properly type the ndarray's shape (_i.e._ we lack variadics).
  As the `~Shape` bound is likelly to change in the future it is recommended to type it as `Any` (for now).
  See https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/16544 for the discussion on shape typing.
* The various functions and methods in numpy currently lack the necessary overloads to the utilize 
  the arrays dtype (for now!). This is considered work for future PRs due to the jobs sheer size.

In some cases:  
```python
Array[int, int, Any]
```
is accepted
```python
T = TypeVarTuple("T")
Array[*T, Any]
```
is not.  Is that consistent with what you see?  There's was a larger note about this in the documentation, but was reduced to [this](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/403029958d001f37815a5e99a58d7a6f85db35f2/numpy/_typing/_add_docstring.py#L162) after another review.

> 2-parameter `Array` alias 

I definitely test-drove this at some point, and we could do it, but the benefits over just `np.ndarray` typing is a bit limited.  I'd like to hear from @BvB93  or @mtsokol before changing the design again, since they have the power to approve.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2221844734/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

>
> In any case, it would mean that somehow the typechecker implicitly inferred that the (variadic) shape type param of Array is bound (when unpacked) to int, even though that shouldn't be possible.

@jorenham  I stand corrected.  Originally, I was using both pyright and mypy on one of the many iterations of the test files for this PR.  I had been able to find some use cases that worked and some that didn't, [but was wary of using TypeVarTuple][orig] for the reason you mentioned - inferring the bound shouldn't be possible.  Apparently, something changed in mypy/pyright (or maybe just in doc tests).  Now
```
ndarray[tuple[Unpack[_ShapeTypeTuple]], dtype[_ScalarType_co]]
```
is being highlighted by my syntax highlighter.  Doc tests now fail, but that might be unrelated.

I want to again float the option of _not_ adding an alias.  The original intent of this PR, variance and bounds, are a definite improvement to the state of numpy typing.  Moreover, choosing an alias without `TypeVarTuple` means that when bounds/variance are added to `TypeVarTuple`, we can't introduce an alias that takes advantage of these features.

You've done a lot of work in numpy typing, is there a chance that you could get PR approval authority?  I'm happy to do whatever a numpy approver asks, I just don't want to circle back to an earlier iteration without a clear path to approval.


[orig]: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26081#issuecomment-2037014185","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2221906829/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

If so, it'll only be a small bit of work to move the documentation to the [ndarray section of typing](https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/typing.html#ndarray), rather than the [API section](https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/typing.html#api), which currently holds the alias.

Note on the CI: the failure is because doc tests are on 3.10, so any `print(npt.Array)` output is not what is expected. +from typing import Any, cast, NewType, TypeVar
+
+import numpy as np
+from typing_extensions import assert_type
+
+Time = NewType("Time", int)
+Series = NewType("Series", int)
+Ax1 = TypeVar("Ax1", bound=int)
+Ax2 = TypeVar("Ax2", bound=int)
+
+arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Time, Series], Any] = np.arange(4.0).reshape((2, 2))
+
+def check_shape(a: np.ndarray[tuple[int, ...], Any]) -> bool: return True
+
+check_shape(arr)
+
+def transpose(
+    a: np.ndarray[tuple[Ax1, Ax2], Any]
+) -> np.ndarray[tuple[Ax2, Ax1], Any]:
+    return cast(np.ndarray[tuple[Ax2, Ax1], Any], np.transpose(a))
+
+assert_type(arr.shape, tuple[Time, Series])
+assert_type(transpose(arr).shape, tuple[Series, {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26845","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26845/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26845/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26845/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26845","id":2389117882,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86OZwu6","number":26845,"title":"ENH: Add docstrings to native type stubs","user":{"login":"rchiodo","id":19672699,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NjcyNjk5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19672699?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rchiodo","html_url":"https://github.com/rchiodo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rchiodo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rchiodo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rchiodo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rchiodo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rchiodo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rchiodo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rchiodo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rchiodo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rchiodo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":true},"labels":[{"id":2124388074,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyMTI0Mzg4MDc0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/Static%20typing","name":"Static typing","color":"fcff6b","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":11,"created_at":"2024-07-03T17:24:14Z","updated_at":"2024-07-25T01:57:18Z","closed_at":"2024-07-24T18:17:03Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

Numpy has a number of native functions as part of its public api, like [numpy.zeros](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.zeros.html). 

The type stubs that ship for these native functions are missing docstrings at the moment. This request is to add the docstring found at runtime in the type stubs.

As a workaround, Pylance generates type stubs for numpy for numpy's native functions. (We run a script that loads all of the functions and asks them for their docstrings) 

Here's an example of the type stub we generated for `numpy.zeros` for 1.0:

```python
def zeros(shape, dtype=..., order=..., *, like=...) -> typing.Any:
    "    Return a new array of given shape and type, filled with zeros.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    shape : int or tuple of ints\n        Shape of the new array, e.g., ``(2, 3)`` or ``2``.\n    dtype : data-type, optional\n        The desired data-type for the array, e.g., `numpy.int8`.  Default is\n        `numpy.float64`.\n    order : {'C', 'F'}, optional, default: 'C'\n        Whether to store multi-dimensional data in row-major\n        (C-style) or column-major (Fortran-style) order in\n        memory.\n    like : array_like, optional\n        Reference object to allow the creation of arrays which are not\n        NumPy arrays. If an array-like passed in as ``like`` supports\n        the ``__array_function__`` protocol, the result will be defined\n        by it. In this case, it ensures the creation of an array object\n        compatible with that passed in via this argument.\n\n        .. versionadded:: 1.20.0\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray\n        Array of zeros with the given shape, dtype, and order.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    zeros_like : Return an array of zeros with shape and type of input.\n    empty : Return a new uninitialized array.\n    ones : Return a new array setting values to one.\n    full : Return a new array of given shape filled with value.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> np.zeros(5)\n    array([ 0.,  0.,  0.,  0.,  0.])\n\n    >>> np.zeros((5,), dtype=int)\n    array([0, 0, 0, 0, 0])\n\n    >>> np.zeros((2, 1))\n    array([[ 0.],\n           [ 0.]])\n\n    >>> s = (2,2)\n    >>> np.zeros(s)\n    array([[ 0.,  0.],\n           [ 0.,  0.]])\n\n    >>> np.zeros((2,), dtype=[('x', 'i4'), ('y', 'i4')]) # custom dtype\n    array([(0, 0), (0, 0)],\n          dtype=[('x', '<i4'), ('y', '<i4')])"
    ...
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26845/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26845/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"not_planned"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2249213348","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26845#issuecomment-2249213348","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26845","id":2249213348,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86GEEWk","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-25T01:57:16Z","updated_at":"2024-07-25T01:57:16Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Reading through the linked discussion, it looks like people had good reasons for docstrings to be available both dynamically and statically.  But GVR made a good point:

> There should be a tool to update the docstrings in stubs before I could recommend duplicating them there

This feels like something that, if enough people are interested, should be a feature of build tooling, rather than implemented independently on a per-repo basis.  Then, this feature could be as simple as adding `stub-docstrings` to `[build-system.requires]`.  Sort of like how `setuptools-scm` proliferated well before the community officially accepted `version` as dynamic metadata and debate continued as to whether VCS info should be part of an sdist.

(FWIW, part of that tool exists: sphinx reads docstrings dynamically and produces a static output)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2249213348/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27032","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27032/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27032/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27032/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27032","id":2428169239,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86QuuwX","number":27032,"title":"TYP: Overlapping overloads with incompatible return types","user":{"login":"jorenham","id":6208662,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYyMDg2NjI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6208662?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham","html_url":"https://github.com/jorenham","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorenham/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":2124388074,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyMTI0Mzg4MDc0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/Static%20typing","name":"Static typing","color":"fcff6b","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-07-24T17:58:48Z","updated_at":"2024-07-25T02:45:12Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

The commonly used `@overload` pattern with `_ArrayLike{}_co` parameter types can lead to unexpected type inferences if the return types are incompatible with one another other.

An example of this is the `numpy.dtype` constructor:
It correctly infers `dtype(bool)` and `dtype(int)` as `dtype[np.bool]` and `dtype[signedinteger[Any]]`, respectively.
But in the case that either `bool` or `int` can be passed, e.g as `dtype(int_co)` with  `int_co: type[bool] | tuple[int]`, the resulting type is inferred as `dtype[signedinteger[Any]]`, which is incompatible with `dtype[bool]`.
The correct type in this case would be `dtype[bool | signedinteger[Any]]`.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
# overlapping_overload_issue.pyi

import numpy as np

x: type[bool]
y: type[int]
z: type[bool] | type[int]

reveal_type(np.dtype(x))  # OK: dtype[bool]
reveal_type(np.dtype(y))  # OK: dtype[signedinteger[Any]]
reveal_type(np.dtype(z))  # WRONG: dtype[signedinteger[Any]]
```

But the last revealed type doesn't include `bool`, i.e. it should have been `dtype[bool | signedinteger[Any]]`.

This isn't limited to only `bool` and `int`; all possible non-trivial unions of `bool`, `int`, `float` or `complex` have this problem.

And this also isn't limited to `dtype.__new__`:
The same issue can be observed with the arithmetic operator methods (e.g. `__add__`) of `numpy.ndarray`, `numpy.bool`, and all subclasses of `numpy.number`, just to name a few.

### Error message:

With Pyright / Pylance, enabling the [`reportOverlappingOverload`](https://github.com/microsoft/pyright/blob/main/docs/configuration.md#reportOverlappingOverload) setting will in such cases result in an error message (unless there's a `# type: ignore` comment), e.g. for `numpy.dtype.__new__` Pylance reports:

```
Overload 1 for "__new__" overlaps overload 51 and returns an incompatible type
Overload 2 for "__new__" overlaps overload 3 and returns an incompatible type
Overload 2 for "__new__" overlaps overload 4 and returns an incompatible type
Overload 2 for "__new__" overlaps overload 5 and returns an incompatible type
```

### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.1.0.dev0+git20240724.3df4283
3.10.12 (main, Mar 22 2024, 16:50:05) [GCC 11.4.0]

### Type-checker version and settings:

basedpyright 1.15.0 (pyright 1.1.373)

### Additional typing packages.

typing-extensions 4.12.2","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27032/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27032/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2249254642","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27032#issuecomment-2249254642","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27032","id":2249254642,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86GEOby","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-25T02:45:11Z","updated_at":"2024-07-25T02:45:11Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Is the solution to use parametrized alias, not union aliases?  e.g.  
```
_ArrayInt_co: TypeAlias = NDArray[np.bool | integer[Any]]
```
would be replaced with:
```python
from typing import TypeVar
Int_co = TypeVar("Int_co", integer[Any], np.bool)
_ArrayInt_co: TypeAlias = NDArray[Int_co]
```

I think the dtype case (for overloads 2, 3, 4, 5) related but a different story though?  It looks like `z` matches [overload 2](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/c118629dd5f08b23d090b4587ef1e899d33f5438/numpy/__init__.pyi#L776).  Letting `z` be a typevar seems to work, and could collapse some of the overloads:

```python
from typing import TypeVar
z = TypeVar("z", bound=type[complex])
def identity(typ: z) -> z: ...

reveal_type(np.dtype(identity(bool))) # OK: dtype[bool]
reveal_type(np.dtype(identity(int)))# OK: dtype[signedinteger[Any]]
reveal_type(np.dtype(identity(float)))  # OK: dtype[floating[_64Bit]]
reveal_type(np.dtype(identity(complex)))  # OK: dtype[complexfloating[_64Bit, _64Bit]]
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2249254642/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1694378013","pull_request_review_id":2203636154,"id":1694378013,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85k_igd","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,41 @@
+``ndarray`` Shapes are now covariant and bound
+---------------------------------------------------------------
+Static typing for ``ndarray`` is a long-term effort that continues 
+with this change.  Arrays are a generic type, parameterized by both 
+shape and data type.  The previous shape-type placeholder could previously be assigned
+any value.  This change restricts it to a tuple of ints, as one would expect
+from using ``ndarray.shape``.  However, this change also allows parameterizing
+shape by a tuple of ``int`` subtypes, which are reflected in the type of
+``ndarray.shape``, and allows functions to statically manipulate axes:
+
+.. code::
+
+   from typing import Any, NewType, TypeVar
+   import numpy as np
+
+   Time = NewType("Time", int)
+   Series = NewType("Series", int)
+   DType = TypeVar("DType", bound=np.dtype)
+   Ax1 = TypeVar("Ax1", bound=int)
+   Ax2 = TypeVar("Ax2", bound=int)
+
+   arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Time, Series], Any] = np.arange(4.0).reshape((2, 2))","path":"doc/release/upcoming_changes/26081.improvement.rst","commit_id":"f37e8f24d08fc96db54e7bbcac0a9ecce50613e5","original_commit_id":"f37e8f24d08fc96db54e7bbcac0a9ecce50613e5","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"If I understand correctly, type declarations are valid so long as they narrow the inferred type.  Since no array creation routines infer the `_ShapeType` from it's arguments,
```
arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Time, Series], Any] = np.arange(4.0).reshape((2, 2))
```
is valid, as is 
```
arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Literal[2], Literal[2]], Any] = np.arange(4.0).reshape((2, 2))
```
because they both narrow the inferred type `ndarray[Any, dtype[floating[Any]]` and obey the type bounds for shape and dtype.  I agree that they can't be combined, as `Literal[Time(2)]` isn't valid.  It's an either or: you can use literal length axes, or you can use `NewType` to say that the axes mean something specific (e.g `Time`, `Series`).  Functions that require one will reject an array declared as the other unless it is later `cast`.

For me, the original point of the PR/covariance was to use `NewType`, as `int` is rarely going to have any true subclasses.  [The example in PEP 646](https://peps.python.org/pep-0646/#motivation) is pretty close to my use case: a library that handles images and videos, and the need for type checking to handle the difference between a gray movie (with axes height, width, frame) from an RGB image (with axes height, width, channel).  E.g. a function `lower_resolution()` should persist its input type and could apply to both, but a function `convert_gray()` that averages the last axis should only accept an RGB image (or RGB movie).

But I agree with your next comment, that an in-depth example is better in the `numpy.typing` docs, rather than release notes.  How about I shrink this example to just show declarations of `int`, `Literal` and `NewType`?

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1694380569","pull_request_review_id":2203639780,"id":1694380569,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85k_jIZ","diff_hunk":"@@ -64,6 +64,31 @@
 ndarray
 ~~~~~~~
 
+The ``ndarray`` type is generic with respect to both its shape and data type.
+The shape parameter is a tuple of ints or subtypes of int.
+
+The data type field is a subclass of ``np.dtype``, usually via parametrization
+with a scalar type: e.g.
+``Float1D = np.ndarray[tuple[int], np.dtype[np.float64]]``.
+
+Array creation cannot yet detect the shape parameter from arguments; it must be
+declared, e.g.:
+
+.. code-block:: python
+
+    > from typing import Literal, NewType
+    > Width = NewType("Width", int)
+    > Height = NewType("Height", int)
+    >
+    > GrayImg = np.ndarray[tuple[Width, Height], np.dtype[np.uint8]]
+    > RGBImg = np.ndarray[tuple[Width, Height, Literal[3]], np.dtype[np.uint8]]
+    >
+    > def colorize(img: GrayImg) -> RGBImg: ...
+    > 
+    > x: GrayImg = np.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]])
+    > assert_type(colorize(x), RGBImg)  # type check passes
+

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1694383395","pull_request_review_id":2203643935,"id":1694383395,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85k_j0j","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,28 @@
+from typing import Any, cast, Literal, NewType, TypeVar
+
+import numpy as np
+from typing_extensions import assert_type
+
+Time = NewType("Time", int)
+Series = NewType("Series", int)
+
+# Test bound
+arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Time, Series], Any] = np.arange(4.0).reshape((2, 2))
+
+Ax1 = TypeVar("Ax1", bound=int)
+Ax2 = TypeVar("Ax2", bound=int)
+def transpose(
+    a: np.ndarray[tuple[Ax1, Ax2], Any]
+) -> np.ndarray[tuple[Ax2, Ax1], Any]:
+    return cast(np.ndarray[tuple[Ax2, Ax1], Any], np.transpose(a))
+
+# Test variance
+assert_type(transpose(arr).shape, tuple[Series, Time])
+
+# Test shape generic
+assert_type(arr.shape, tuple[Time, Series])
+
+# Test literal
+arr2: np.ndarray[tuple[Literal[1], Literal[2]], Any] = np.arange(2).reshape((1, 2))
+assert_type(arr2.shape, tuple[Literal[1], Literal[2]])
+assert_type(transpose(arr2).shape, tuple[Literal[2], Literal[1]])","path":"numpy/typing/tests/data/pass/shape.py","commit_id":"f37e8f24d08fc96db54e7bbcac0a9ecce50613e5","original_commit_id":"f37e8f24d08fc96db54e7bbcac0a9ecce50613e5","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Oooh, interesting point, and you're right.  I was less familiar with using `Literal`, but apparently it is treated as a subtype in a way "identical to how we handle NewTypes."

I think both do handle covariance of `tuple` itself, because tuple is covariant with respect to _its_ arguments - `tuple[Time, Literal[2]]` is a subtype of `tuple`.  But you're right it makes sense to add a test using a real tuple {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1694413045","pull_request_review_id":2203685027,"id":1694413045,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85k_rD1","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,28 @@
+from typing import Any, cast, Literal, NewType, TypeVar
+
+import numpy as np
+from typing_extensions import assert_type
+
+Time = NewType("Time", int)
+Series = NewType("Series", int)
+
+# Test bound
+arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Time, Series], Any] = np.arange(4.0).reshape((2, 2))
+
+Ax1 = TypeVar("Ax1", bound=int)
+Ax2 = TypeVar("Ax2", bound=int)
+def transpose(
+    a: np.ndarray[tuple[Ax1, Ax2], Any]
+) -> np.ndarray[tuple[Ax2, Ax1], Any]:
+    return cast(np.ndarray[tuple[Ax2, Ax1], Any], np.transpose(a))
+
+# Test variance
+assert_type(transpose(arr).shape, tuple[Series, Time])
+
+# Test shape generic
+assert_type(arr.shape, tuple[Time, Series])
+
+# Test literal
+arr2: np.ndarray[tuple[Literal[1], Literal[2]], Any] = np.arange(2).reshape((1, 2))
+assert_type(arr2.shape, tuple[Literal[1], Literal[2]])

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1697370927","pull_request_review_id":2208348171,"id":1697370927,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85lK9Mv","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,28 @@
+from typing import Any, cast, Literal, NewType, TypeVar
+
+import numpy as np
+from typing_extensions import assert_type
+
+Time = NewType("Time", int)
+Series = NewType("Series", int)
+
+# Test bound
+arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Time, Series], Any] = np.arange(4.0).reshape((2, 2))
+
+Ax1 = TypeVar("Ax1", bound=int)
+Ax2 = TypeVar("Ax2", bound=int)
+def transpose(
+    a: np.ndarray[tuple[Ax1, Ax2], Any]
+) -> np.ndarray[tuple[Ax2, Ax1], Any]:
+    return cast(np.ndarray[tuple[Ax2, Ax1], Any], np.transpose(a))
+
+# Test variance
+assert_type(transpose(arr).shape, tuple[Series, Time])
+
+# Test shape generic
+assert_type(arr.shape, tuple[Time, Series])
+
+# Test literal
+arr2: np.ndarray[tuple[Literal[1], Literal[2]], Any] = np.arange(2).reshape((1, 2))
+assert_type(arr2.shape, tuple[Literal[1], Literal[2]])
+assert_type(transpose(arr2).shape, tuple[Literal[2], Literal[1]])","path":"numpy/typing/tests/data/pass/shape.py","commit_id":"05fd075e80c9f56ccd3245595b3820a323a9d250","original_commit_id":"f37e8f24d08fc96db54e7bbcac0a9ecce50613e5","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Ah! {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1697391285","pull_request_review_id":2208379712,"id":1697391285,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85lLCK1","diff_hunk":"@@ -64,6 +64,31 @@
 ndarray
 ~~~~~~~
 
+The ``ndarray`` type is generic with respect to both its shape and data type.
+The shape parameter is a tuple of ints or subtypes of int.
+
+The data type field is a subclass of ``np.dtype``, usually via parametrization
+with a scalar type: e.g.
+``Float1D = np.ndarray[tuple[int], np.dtype[np.float64]]``.
+
+Array creation cannot yet detect the shape parameter from arguments; it must be
+declared, e.g.:
+
+.. code-block:: python
+
+    > from typing import Literal, NewType
+    > Width = NewType("Width", int)
+    > Height = NewType("Height", int)
+    >
+    > GrayImg = np.ndarray[tuple[Width, Height], np.dtype[np.uint8]]
+    > RGBImg = np.ndarray[tuple[Width, Height, Literal[3]], np.dtype[np.uint8]]
+    >
+    > def colorize(img: GrayImg) -> RGBImg: ...
+    > 
+    > x: GrayImg = np.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]])
+    > assert_type(colorize(x), RGBImg)  # type check passes
+
+","path":"numpy/typing/__init__.py","commit_id":"05fd075e80c9f56ccd3245595b3820a323a9d250","original_commit_id":"f37e8f24d08fc96db54e7bbcac0a9ecce50613e5","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"That all makes sense, but until a week ago, I wasn't really planning on working more deeply with numpy typing.  I was exactly the user you described above: one who sees this change as a feature that allows me to shape-type my own functions, even if I have to `cast` the output.

I'm ok removing the docs for now, though.  I agree it could be confusing for users who, as soon as they do something like `arr + 1`, lose the shape type information. {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1697738895","pull_request_review_id":2208916157,"id":1697738895,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85lMXCP","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,28 @@
+from typing import Any, cast, Literal, NewType, TypeVar
+
+import numpy as np
+from typing_extensions import assert_type
+
+Time = NewType("Time", int)
+Series = NewType("Series", int)
+
+# Test bound
+arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Time, Series], Any] = np.arange(4.0).reshape((2, 2))
+
+Ax1 = TypeVar("Ax1", bound=int)
+Ax2 = TypeVar("Ax2", bound=int)
+def transpose(
+    a: np.ndarray[tuple[Ax1, Ax2], Any]
+) -> np.ndarray[tuple[Ax2, Ax1], Any]:
+    return cast(np.ndarray[tuple[Ax2, Ax1], Any], np.transpose(a))","path":"numpy/typing/tests/data/reveal/shape.pyi","commit_id":"05fd075e80c9f56ccd3245595b3820a323a9d250","original_commit_id":"05fd075e80c9f56ccd3245595b3820a323a9d250","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Hey purepani, yes, the test passes. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28151","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28151/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28151/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28151/events","html_url":"https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28151","id":2270037735,"node_id":"I_kwDOABUios6HTgbn","number":28151,"title":"[Bug]: GridSpec do not type check as Iterable in zip","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-04-29T21:41:02Z","updated_at":"2024-05-01T01:43:42Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Bug summary

`GridSpec` objects are iterable because they have a `__getitem__` method.  However, they are not a [`collections.abc.Iterable`](1):

> \[`Iterable`\] does not detect classes that iterate with the [`__getitem__()`](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__getitem__) method.

This means that they do not type check in `zip`, at least according to [typeshed](2)

[1]: https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.abc.html#collections.abc.Iterable
[2]: https://github.com/python/typeshed/blob/78a86de5d966fe403c4184fb4a795e7fb559214b/stdlib/builtins.pyi#L1714

### Code for reproduction

```Python
from matplotlib.gridspec import GridSpec

gs = GridSpec(3, 3)
counter = range(9)
for cell in gs:
    print(cell)

for i, cell in zip(counter, gs):
    print(cell)
```


### Actual outcome

Code runs, but type type checking says that GridSpec is not Iterable

### Expected outcome

Type checks OK

It doesn't look like the community is going to come to a consensus soon on whether it's a
* typeshed responsibility (by integrating the `_GetItemIterable` to anything that accepts `collections.abc.Iterable`), 
* type checker's responsibility (by special casing this behavior),
* downstream package's responsibility (by implementing explicit `__iter__`).

I'm slightly in favor of the explicit `__iter__`, if only from the limited perspective of "I'm a user and this is the fastest way to solve the problem for users like me."","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/comments/2087850003/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

So I'm coming to believe that any form of `npt.Array` that uses `TypeVarTuple` may have to be delayed for another PR.  I'm chasing around failed tests, and while things are hunky dory for 3.11 and 3.12, something always goes wrong with 3.10

* Typing extensions is unavailable in some CI environments, meaning `npt.Array` has to be assigned a different value in <3.11
* Typing tests are executed in 3.10, 3.11, and 3.12.  Even if we guard shape311.py with `if sys.version_info ...`, 3.10 still checks those files
* If it can't handle a variadic number of parameters, for Array, it fails when it tries to run the tests (separate from testing type checking, all of the test files need to run)
* For some reason, even with quoted types (delayed evaluation of annotations) it still fails when `Array = NDArray`","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2097298175/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1550021651","pull_request_review_id":1977263986,"id":1550021651,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85cY3QT","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,44 @@
+``ndarray`` Shapes are now covariant and bound
+---------------------------------------------------------------
+Static shape typing for ``ndarray`` is a long-term effort that continues 
+with this change.  Array shape can be described with a tuple of ints, but
+both ``TypeVar`` and ``TypeVarTuple`` provide some useful functionality.
+The former allows bounds and type variance, whereas the latter allows
+unpacking.  ``ndarray`` is built with a ``TypeVar``, and this change
+provides the appropriate bounds and variance. Ok, so I added some tests for `ndarray`, and `npt.Array`, but I put them in `test_success` rather than `test_reveal` - I was unsure of the difference.  Larger problem, the `Array` ones fail.  The problem is that `Array` unpacks a `TypeVarTuple` into a bound `TypeVar`.  pyright doesn't see a problem with it, but mypy does.  The non-tuple equivalent:

```python
from typing import TypeVar

T = TypeVar("T", bound=str)
S = TypeVar("S")

def foo(a: T) -> T: ...
def bar(b: S, c: S) -> T:
    return foo(b)  # type error since object is not a str
```

Unfortunately, pyright also accepts things like `npt.Array[str, str, np.uint8]`

As a result, I've reverted the tests for `npt.Array` (so that people can play with it if they'd like), but left in `npt.Array` as written.  It seems like, **until `TypeVarTuple` supports binding and variance, there's two options**:
a) Change `npt.Array` to take a [tuple shape, not a variadic one](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26081#discussion_r1535554708).
b) Decide the current API is desired, ship as currently written, refer bug reports to `np.ndarray`, and use the experience to motivate further work on PEP 646.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2037014185/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28151","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28151/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28151/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28151/events","html_url":"https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28151","id":2270037735,"node_id":"I_kwDOABUios6HTgbn","number":28151,"title":"[Bug]: GridSpec are not Iterable","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-04-29T21:41:02Z","updated_at":"2024-04-29T21:41:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Bug summary

`GridSpec` objects are iterable because they have a `__getitem__` method.  However, they are not a [`collections.abc.Iterable`](1):

> \[`Iterable`\] does not detect classes that iterate with the [`__getitem__()`](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__getitem__) method.

This means that they do not type check in `zip`, at least according to [typeshed](2)

[1]: https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.abc.html#collections.abc.Iterable
[2]: https://github.com/python/typeshed/blob/78a86de5d966fe403c4184fb4a795e7fb559214b/stdlib/builtins.pyi#L1714

### Code for reproduction

```Python
from matplotlib.gridspec import GridSpec

gs = GridSpec(3, 3)
counter = range(9)
for cell in gs:
    print(cell)

for i, cell in zip(counter, gs):
    print(cell)
```


### Actual outcome

Code runs, but type type checking says that GridSpec is not Iterable

### Expected outcome

Type checks OK

not sure if you directly annotated `ndarray` for readability, but the current syntax uses a `TypeVar` for the shape argument so that methods that preserve shape can reuse it. I.e. the current syntax isn't
```python
class ndarray[~Shape: Any, +DType: dtype[Any]]:
```
but
```python
_ShapeType = TypeVar("_ShapeType", bound=Any)
_DType_co = TypeVar("_DType_co", covariant=True, bound=dtype[Any])

class ndarray(_ArrayOrScalarCommon, Generic[_ShapeType, _DType_co]):
```
If you're talking about modifying `_ShapeType`, I def agree! #25729


> in practice shape will be typed as a @property

[Isn't it already?
](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/6059db15f06555b246f3743db3152050b50722b1/numpy/__init__.pyi#L1530
)

> The annotations of many methods, operators, ufuncs, and other functions can now be made more specific... For functions with a more dynamic shape mapping, this might not be possible

I'd also add that stepping down this path could help illuminate the right way to resolve a lot of the "left for the future" stuff in PEP 646.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2006819905/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I'm looking to parametrize a numpy array with a type variable.  Not sure if this is meant to be implemented, and thus a bug, or not-yet-implemented, and thus a feature request.  The following code is flagged by Pylance:

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
from numpy.typing import NDArray
from typing import TypeVar

T = TypeVar("T")
GridsearchResult = Annotated[NDArray[T], "(n_metrics, n_plot_axis)"]
```


### Error message:

```shell
Could not specialize type "NDArray[_ScalarType_co@NDArray]"
  Type "T@GridsearchResult" cannot be assigned to type "generic"
    "object" is incompatible with "generic"
```

### Context for the issue:

My gridsearch code returns a tuple of arrays, both 2D of the same size.  One return array carries the maxes of a larger array along several axes, the other return array carries the argmax along those indices.  So the two types are:

```python
Annotated[NDArray[np.float64], "(n_metrics, plot_axis_length)"]
Annotated[NDArray[np.dtype("i,i,i,i")], "(n_metrics, plot_axis_length)"]
```
Here, the length of the indexing tuple is variable/determined later, but I would like a function signature akin to (ignoring the size annotation for now):

```python
def maxes_and_argmaxes[T, U](arr: NDArray[T], axes: tuple[int, ...]): -> tuple[NDArray[T], NDArray[U]]
```
Here, U would be the variadic tuple of ints dtype.

I admit I'm a bit new to type variables, so this may not be the right approach.  But I imagine the correct final form, if I want to `Annotate` the `NDArray`s is

```python
T = TypeVar("T")
GridsearchResult = Annotated[NDArray[T], "(n_metrics, n_plot_axis)"]

def maxes_and_argmaxes[T, U](
     arr: NDArray[T], axes: tuple[int, ...]
): -> tuple[GridsearchResult[T], GridsearchResult[U]]
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25153/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25153/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2011837685","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25153#issuecomment-2011837685","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25153","id":2011837685,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP8536jT1","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-03-21T10:16:48Z","updated_at":"2024-03-21T10:16:48Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"@mscheltienne try [`np.floating`](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.scalars.html#numpy.floating), a subtype of `np.generic`. > The `ndarray` class was made generic prior to the development of `TypeVarTuple`

Oh, sorry, I was just going off of git blame vs PEP date, and trying to fill in some context for readers who had heard of `TypeVarTuple` why this PR was moving away from that option:  changing `_ShapeType` to covariant pre-empts a PR changing it to a `TypeVarTuple` until the latter supports variance.  There's also more of my rationale at the end of this comment.  Would the following language work?

```
Static shape typing for `ndarray` is a long-term effort that continues 
with this change.  While shape is a variadic type variable, this change
chooses to keep `TypeVar` rather than `TypeVarTuple`, as the latter
does not yet allow bounds or variance.  This choice allows users
to type check functions that attempt to specify the shape parameter
as a `NewType`:
```

> And while type var tuples do not support a bound, type vars _do_

I'm 99% on board with bounding to `tuple[int, ...]`, as in your code and @jorenham's suggestion.  My only reservation is that I don't know enough about structured array data types.  E.g., in a length-3 structured array with two fields (or a record array of the same), would the shape typevar be `tuple[int, int]`?  Or is the dtype typevar responsible for the field length, meaning the shape typevar is `tuple[int]`?  Because I don't know, I limited this PR just towards variance, not bounds.  But if it's desired, I'm more than happy to add the bound.

> Another option is to convert the first type var of ``ndarray`` into a type var tuple, but this has the very drawback that (to the extent of my knowledge) there's no way of representing a tuple with an unknown/arbitrary length (aka array dimensions).

I thought `TypeVarTuple`s are always arbitrary length before they're narrowed/resolved?  But by gut feeling alone, `TypeVarTuple` seem less useful than `TypeVar("Shape", bound=tuple[int, ...])` until more of the TBDs from PEP 646 are resolved (e.g. [manipulating individual types][1] and [shape arithmetic][2]), especially because `Tuple` now also supports unpacking types.  I also prefer the behavior of `tuple` to `TypeVarTuple` when dealing with [unknown type parameters][3].  That is, I prefer type checking to show more shape errors, even if it requires peppering my code with `cast`.

[1]: https://peps.python.org/pep-0646/#overloads-for-accessing-individual-types
[2]: https://peps.python.org/pep-0646/#shape-arithmetic
[3]: +Shapes are now covariant
+------------------------
+When ``ndarray`` was made generic, more details and implementation were
+left to future development.  While it was recognized that shape was inherently
+a tuple (and thus covariant), development towards ``TypeVarTuple`` does not
+yet allow bounds or variance.  This change allows user code to type check in
+functions that attempt to specify the shape parameter:
+
+.. code::
+
+   from typing import NewType, Any
+   import numpy as np
+
+   Time = NewType("Time", int)
+   Series = NewType("Series", int)
+
+   arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Time, Series], Any] = np.arange(4.0).reshape((2, 2))","path":"doc/release/upcoming_changes/26081.improvement.rst","commit_id":"fa3cdb479b5821b59890981ce5da67a5ee01e485","original_commit_id":"fa3cdb479b5821b59890981ce5da67a5ee01e485","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Happy to add an alias!  My only consideration is whether the alias should expect grouping dimensions, e.g.:
```python
FloatArray2D = npt.ShapedArray[tuple[int, int], np.float64]
```
I slightly agree with @vnmabus's [comment](1): "I think it [requiring dimensions to be grouped] would make it more consistent with functions and classes that need to take more than one shape".  That said, I'll defer to you if you want the flatter version of `ShapedArray`?

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1535554708","pull_request_review_id":1954762918,"id":1535554708,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85bhrSU","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,21 @@
+Shapes are now covariant
+------------------------
+When ``ndarray`` was made generic, more details and implementation were
+left to future development.  While it was recognized that shape was inherently
+a tuple (and thus covariant), development towards ``TypeVarTuple`` does not
+yet allow bounds or variance.  This change allows user code to type check in
+functions that attempt to specify the shape parameter:
+
+.. code::
+
+   from typing import NewType, Any
+   import numpy as np
+
+   Time = NewType("Time", int)
+   Series = NewType("Series", int)
+
+   arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Time, Series], Any] = np.arange(4.0).reshape((2, 2))","path":"doc/release/upcoming_changes/26081.improvement.rst","commit_id":"fa3cdb479b5821b59890981ce5da67a5ee01e485","original_commit_id":"fa3cdb479b5821b59890981ce5da67a5ee01e485","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I think there's going to be a lot of opinions on variadic shape vs tuple shape and shape first vs dtype first.  Here's the pros/cons I've thought through so far:

#### variadic shape, dtype first:
* Follows example in PEP 646
* Similar to `foo(arg1, *args)`
  * People using `NDArray` are used to dtype first
* Shorter
#### tuple shape, dtype last:
* Similar to funcs like `np.ones(shape: tuple[int, ...], dtype: dtype)`
  * People who haven't used static typing are used to this order
* Same as `ndarray` typing
  * People who've already been static shape typing have to use this syntax
 
Polled my office and people unanimously supported the second.

**variadic shape, dtype last** seems like a decent compromise, and it's what I'll commit. I'm happy to sit on the PR and change it back if a better consensus is desired, but I've overthought myself to a point of analysis paralysis.  I've coded all these options up, hopped back and forth, and test-drove them in a few use cases (and they all seem to work fine)

> On a related note; how about naming it Array instead of ShapedArray?


Haha yeah good point.  ~thefacebook.com~ facebook.com :smile: {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1535569830","pull_request_review_id":1954787735,"id":1535569830,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85bhu-m","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,21 @@
+Shapes are now covariant
+------------------------
+When ``ndarray`` was made generic, more details and implementation were
+left to future development.  While it was recognized that shape was inherently
+a tuple (and thus covariant), development towards ``TypeVarTuple`` does not
+yet allow bounds or variance.  This change allows user code to type check in
+functions that attempt to specify the shape parameter:
+
+.. code::
+
+   from typing import NewType, Any
+   import numpy as np
+
+   Time = NewType("Time", int)
+   Series = NewType("Series", int)
+
+   arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Time, Series], Any] = np.arange(4.0).reshape((2, 2))","path":"doc/release/upcoming_changes/26081.improvement.rst","commit_id":"fa3cdb479b5821b59890981ce5da67a5ee01e485","original_commit_id":"fa3cdb479b5821b59890981ce5da67a5ee01e485","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Also a neat lesson learned here: 
```python
TupType = TypeVar("TupType", covariant=True, bound=tuple[int, ...])
TypeTup = TypeVarTuple("TypeTup")

class Foo[TupType]: ...
Bar = Foo[tuple[*TypeTup]]

def myfunc(a: Bar): ...
```
^ an effective way of enforcing type variance and bounds on a `TypeVarTuple`.  Combines (some of) the advantages of {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1535653593","pull_request_review_id":1954915564,"id":1535653593,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85biDbZ","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,21 @@
+Shapes are now covariant
+------------------------
+When ``ndarray`` was made generic, more details and implementation were
+left to future development.  While it was recognized that shape was inherently
+a tuple (and thus covariant), development towards ``TypeVarTuple`` does not
+yet allow bounds or variance.  This change allows user code to type check in
+functions that attempt to specify the shape parameter:
+
+.. code::
+
+   from typing import NewType, Any
+   import numpy as np
+
+   Time = NewType("Time", int)
+   Series = NewType("Series", int)
+
+   arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Time, Series], Any] = np.arange(4.0).reshape((2, 2))","path":"doc/release/upcoming_changes/26081.improvement.rst","commit_id":"fa3cdb479b5821b59890981ce5da67a5ee01e485","original_commit_id":"fa3cdb479b5821b59890981ce5da67a5ee01e485","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Actually,
