Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-01-14T18:26:33Z","updated_at":"2024-01-14T18:26:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

The `numpy.linalg` [docs](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.linalg.solve.html)  claim, "Returned shape is identical to b [the second argument]."  It isn't, though.

e.g.
```python
import numpy as np

A = np.random.normal(size=(2,3,3))
b = np.random.normal(size=(1,3))

x = np.linalg.solve(A, b)
x.shape
```
gives (2, 3).  More generally, the shape of `b` can be "{(…, M,), (…, M, K)}", which I think is what requires `linalg` to break the normal rules for broadcasting, which would begin from the right and pads missing axes.  E.g.
```python
A = np.random.normal(size=(2,2,3,3))
b = np.random.normal(size=(1,3))

x = np.linalg.solve(A, b)
```
```
ValueError: solve: Input operand 1 has a mismatch in its core dimension 0, with gufunc signature (m,m),(m,n)->(m,n) (size 1 is different from 3)
```
I would expect the (1,) to be broadcast to (2, 2) as it would in normal array broadcasting, but `linalg` doesn't expand missing axes.  In another case, if I want b to be of shape `(…, M, K)`, I would expect the following to have shape (3, 3, 3)
```python
A = np.random.normal(size=(3,3,3))
b = np.random.normal(size=(3,3))

x = np.linalg.solve(A, b)
```
but it has shape (3, 3).

So it feels clear what's happening here.  

### Idea or request for content:

It would be great if the wording 
> the last 1 or 2 dimensions of a multidimensional array are interpreted as vectors or matrices, as appropriate for each operation.

in the [linalg section on broadcasting](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/routines.linalg.html#linear-algebra-on-several-matrices-at-once) could be made more explicit that _the `...` are broadcast from the left without padding missing axes_. So the logic is:
b needs at least as many axes as A
if b.nim == A.ndim: b interpreted left style ({.., M})
if b.ndim == A.ndim +1: b interpreted as right style ({..., M, K})
if b.ndim > A.ndim +1: b interpreted as right style ({..., M, K}) and A is left-padded to match.

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-01-14T18:26:33Z","updated_at":"2024-01-17T13:06:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

The `numpy.linalg` [docs](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.linalg.solve.html)  claim, "Returned shape is identical to b [the second argument]."  It isn't, though.  Returned `ndim` is identical to b.

e.g.
```python
import numpy as np

A = np.random.normal(size=(2,3,3))
b = np.random.normal(size=(1,3))

x = np.linalg.solve(A, b)
x.shape
```
gives (2, 3).  More generally, the shape of `b` can be "{(…, M,), (…, M, K)}", which I think is what requires `linalg` to break the normal rules for broadcasting, which would begin from the right and pad missing axes.  E.g.
```python
A = np.random.normal(size=(2,2,3,3))
b = np.random.normal(size=(1,3))

x = np.linalg.solve(A, b)
```
```
ValueError: solve: Input operand 1 has a mismatch in its core dimension 0, with gufunc signature (m,m),(m,n)->(m,n) (size 1 is different from 3)
```
I would expect the (1,) to be broadcast to (2, 2) as it would in normal array broadcasting, but `linalg` doesn't expand missing axes.  In another case, if I want b to be of shape `(…, M, K)`, I would expect the following to have shape (3, 3, 3)
```python
A = np.random.normal(size=(3,3,3))
b = np.random.normal(size=(3,3))

x = np.linalg.solve(A, b)
```
but it has shape (3, 3).

So it feels clear what's happening here.  

### Idea or request for content:

It would be great if the wording 
> the last 1 or 2 dimensions of a multidimensional array are interpreted as vectors or matrices, as appropriate for each operation.

in the [linalg section on broadcasting](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/routines.linalg.html#linear-algebra-on-several-matrices-at-once) could be made more explicit that _the `...` are broadcast from the left without padding missing axes_.  ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25583/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25583/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1895774843","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25583#issuecomment-1895774843","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25583","id":1895774843,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP85w_zp7","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-17T13:06:24Z","updated_at":"2024-01-17T13:06:24Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Ok thank you, the examples in there give a better sense of the issue.  

@rgommers The point in NEP 56 **does** fix this, as choosing whether `b` is a stack of matrices or vectors would no longer depend upon conditions that occur in broadcasting. I think that's the crux of the issue.

> The broadcasting behavior of solve is difficult to summarize; I don't think docs are a very useful way to address that, because fundamentally the documented specification is ambiguous

At the very least, the statement, "Returned shape is identical to `b`" is wrong, and probably should be removed.  How about "Return shape convention matches `b`." {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25729","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25729/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25729/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25729/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25729","id":2108152035,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP859p9jj","number":25729,"title":"ENH: _ShapeType should be covariant","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-01-30T15:31:33Z","updated_at":"2024-01-30T15:31:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

I believe [`_ShapeType`](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/2a52c5778ceb5be4bdd7e54925d87f16f748f9d2/numpy/__init__.pyi#L1383) should be covariant.  For instance, the following code does not typecheck because `_ShapeType` is invariant:

```
from typing import NewType
import numpy as np
from numpy.typing import NBitBase

Time = NewType("Time", int)
Series = NewType("Series", int)
AnyFloat = np.dtype[np.floating[NBitBase]]

arr: np.ndarray[tuple[Time, Series], AnyFloat] = np.arange(3.0)

def foo(a: np.ndarray[tuple[int, ...], AnyFloat]) -> None: ...

foo(arr)
```

I don't think there's any problems making it covariant, because I would assume it will be replaced by `TypeVarTuple` ([PEP 646](https://peps.python.org/pep-0646/)), and tuples are covariant.  In the interim, there's a few functions that change the shape of the array, such as `__getitem__`, but it seems those return `ndarray[Any,]` except in the overload:
```python
    @overload
    def __getitem__(self: NDArray[void], key: list[str]) -> ndarray[_ShapeType, _dtype[void]]: ...
```
I'm not sure what situation this is handling, since I've never seen `self` typed.

There's also the `chararray`, `memmap`, `matrix`,  `recordarray`, `MaskedArray`, and `MaskedRecord` types, but their methods also appear to return `Any` as the shape of any return that isn't the same shape.

Would you mind if I PR'd this and saw if it passes type checking?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25729/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25729/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

A desk neighbor mentioned that they too had had the same problem building matplotlib to make a PR in the past.

I had assumed I was the only developer trying to work on matplotlib that ran into the qhull issue.  But randomly meeting someone else, in-person, on a different network, in a different country, only a month later makes me think that a lot of would-be developers on academic/corporate networks are likely getting stopped or burdened by this issue.

My colleague believed it was because IT blocked qhull, perhaps because it was HTTP and not HTTPS.  OTOH, the authors of qhull mentioned that the qhull server [didn't support range requests](https://github.com/qhull/qhull/issues/132#issuecomment-1789919866), in case that had been the issue.

I have a branch moving the build to qhull's git-tagged 8.0.2 tarball. Bug summary

In a development install, running `pytest -s` hangs indefinitely.  This causes VSCode's python test explorer to also hang indefinitely as it tries to populate the list of tests (it seems like it ignores settings.json for the command to populate the list).  The behavior occurs when running a single test, e.g.

### Code for reproduction

```python
pytest -s --co lib/matplotlib/tests/test_legend.py::test_text_nohandler_warning
```



### Actual outcome


```
pytest-xvfb could not find Xvfb. You can install it to prevent windows from being shown.
======================================== test session starts ========================================
platform linux -- Python 3.10.12, pytest-7.4.2, pluggy-1.3.0
rootdir: /home/xenophon/github/matplotlib
configfile: pytest.ini
plugins: xdist-3.3.1, rerunfailures-12.0, timeout-2.2.0, cov-4.1.0, xvfb-3.0.0
collecting ... 
```
(keeps collecting indefinitely)

### Expected outcome
Detects all tests

### Additional information

_No response_

### Operating system

Linux 6.2.0-34-generic; Ubuntu 22.04.1

### Matplotlib Version

dev build on current master, `fcd5bb1a2b`

### Matplotlib Backend

agg

### Python version

3.10.12

### Jupyter version

N/A

### Installation

Closes #27159.  As mentioned there, the Qhull server is HTTP (not HTTPS).  While that's not a security problem in theory (we know the hash to expect), in practice corporate/academic may servers block the download.  This includes [eduroam](https://eduroam.org/), used by many large research universities.  Furthermore, as @tacaswell mentioned, rather than hitting Qhull's server with every build, it's better to rely on github. (IIUC - lmk if I'm misrepresenting!).

PR summary
Closes #27159.  As mentioned there, the Qhull server is HTTP (not HTTPS).  While that's not a security problem in theory (we know the hash to expect), in practice corporate/academic may servers block the download.  This includes [eduroam](https://eduroam.org/), used by many large research universities.  Furthermore, as @tacaswell mentioned, rather than hitting Qhull's server with every build, it's better to rely on github. (IIUC - lmk if I'm misrepresenting!).

The original issue got a lot of attention, but appeared resolved when I moved to a new network.  When I met a new colleague who mentioned the same issue, I reopened the issue - understand if this needs more discussion beforehand.

PR summary
Closes #27159.  As mentioned there, the Qhull server is HTTP (not HTTPS).  While that's not a security problem in theory (we know the hash to expect), in practice corporate/academic may servers block the download.  This includes [eduroam](https://eduroam.org/), used by many large research universities.  Furthermore, as @tacaswell mentioned, rather than hitting Qhull's server with every build, it's better to rely on github. (IIUC - lmk if I'm misrepresenting!).

The original issue got a lot of attention, but appeared resolved when I moved to a new network.  When I met a new colleague who mentioned the same issue, I reopened the issue - understand if this needs more discussion beforehand.

`np.dtype("i,") == np.dtype("i")` (note the comma).  Is this designed?  It feels like the former should result in a structure with one field, whereas the latter should result in a scalar.  Same as how python handles parentheses in tuples, i.e. `(val)` is interpreted differently than `(val,)`

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

arr = np.empty((1,), dtype="i,")
arr[0] = (5,)
```


### Error message:

```shell
TypeError: int() argument must be a string, a bytes-like object or a real number, not 'tuple'

The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
ValueError: setting an array element with a sequence.
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

python :`'3.10.11 (main, Apr  5 2023, 14:15:30) [GCC 7.5.0]'`
numpy: `1.24.1`

### Context for the issue:

I'm implementing a version of `np.argmax` that accepts a `axis: None or int or tuple of ints` in the way that `np.max` does.  But I also unravel the index from an into into a tuple.  Since I don't know the length of the `axis` tuple beforehand, I choose the dtype as `arr.ndim * "i,"`.


I know there's other ways of [constructing a dtype](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.dtypes.html) if I manually name the fields `"f0", "f1", ...`. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/9283","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/9283/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/9283/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/9283/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/9283","id":237542571,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWUyMzc1NDI1NzE=","number":9283,"title":"Request: argmax2d","user":{"login":"mortonjt","id":4184797,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQxODQ3OTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4184797?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortonjt","html_url":"https://github.com/mortonjt","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortonjt/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortonjt/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortonjt/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortonjt/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortonjt/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortonjt/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortonjt/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortonjt/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mortonjt/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":14,"created_at":"2017-06-21T13:54:06Z","updated_at":"2023-11-11T10:00:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It would be really nice if there was a convenience function to perform an argmax over a 2D array and return the row and column indexes of the maximum.

I'm often finding myself reusing the following code

```python
def argmax2d(X):
    n, m = X.shape
    x_ = np.ravel(X)
    k = np.argmax(x_)
    i, j = k // m, k % m
    return i, j
```
While it is simple, its opaque enough where I have to consistently look this function up.
It would be nice if this was already available in numpy.

Would there be any objections to opening up a PR in numpy to have this functionality readily available?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/9283/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/9283/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1806770284","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/9283#issuecomment-1806770284","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/9283","id":1806770284,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP85rsSBs","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-11-11T10:00:02Z","updated_at":"2023-11-11T10:00:02Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"It's an old issue to be sure, but this came up for me today. #27145

Also add test_legend_nolabels_warning()

## PR summary
After discussion in the linked issue, it was determined that `warnings.warn()` was better than `log.warning()` for alerting users that we can't draw a legend if there are no labels.  This PR implements the change from `log.warning()` to `warnings.warn` (actually, `_api.warn_external()`)

#27145

Also add test_legend_nolabels_warning()

## PR summary
After discussion in the linked issue, it was determined that `warnings.warn()` was better than `log.warning()` for alerting users that we can't draw a legend if there are no labels.  This PR implements the change from `log.warning()` to `warnings.warn` (actually, `_api.warn_external()`)

#27145

Also add test_legend_nolabels_warning()

## PR summary
After discussion in the linked issue, it was determined that `warnings.warn()` was better than `log.warning()` for alerting users that we can't draw a legend if there are no labels.  This PR implements the change from `log.warning()` to `warnings.warn` (actually, `_api.warn_external()`)

feature","color":"02d7e1","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-10-19T13:51:10Z","updated_at":"2023-10-19T15:23:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Problem

When I receive this issue, it's hard to debug and find the specific call to `ax.legend()`  if I can't raise Warnings to Exceptions.  I sometimes nest plotting functions in order to draw the same plot different ways or arrange plots in a composite figure, depending upon the presentation.

MWE and Full message:

```python
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
import warnings

warnings.simplefilter("error", category=Warning)
plt.plot([1,2,3])
plt.legend()
```
(no error, plot appears)
> No artists with labels found to put in legend.  Note that artists whose label start with an underscore are ignored when legend() is called with no argument.

### Proposed solution

Make this a true `UserWarning`.  Currently, it's a `logging.LogRecord` of level=`logging.Warning`.  
```python
log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
...
log.warning(<this message>)
```
**Additional pro:** In the future, it would be easier to find and work on this warning if it is, in fact, a true `Warning`.

**Con:** It won't be handled by the logging infrastructure.  But legend.py uses the default module logger, and the only `LogRecord` that it emits is this one.  The only situation I could think of where this might matter is if a user has some override of global logging setup (by calling `logging.setLoggerClass()`) but does not apply a similar philosophy to capturing warnings in STDERR.

> the user can suppress the warning by either adding a label or removing the legend call.

sounds like the standard, "the issue is avoidable and the client application should be modified to eliminate the warning."  I can't think of a situation where a user would legitimately intend to call `.legend()` without labels. Also add test_legend_nolabels_warning()

## PR summary
After discussion in the linked issue, it was determined that `warnings.warn()` was better than `log.warning()` for alerting users that we can't draw a legend if there are no labels.  This PR implements the change from `log.warning()` to `warnings.warn` (actually, `_api.warn_external()`)

## PR summary
After discussion in the linked issue, it was determined that `warnings.warn()` was better than `log.warning()` for alerting users that we can't draw a legend if there are no labels.  This PR implements the change from `log.warning()` to `warnings.warn` (actually, `_api.warn_external()`)

warning","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":115014,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTUwMTQ=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/labels/New%20feature","name":"New feature","color":"02d7e1","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2023-10-19T13:51:10Z","updated_at":"2023-10-23T13:30:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Problem

When I receive this issue, it's hard to debug and find the specific call to `ax.legend()`  if I can't raise Warnings to Exceptions.  I sometimes nest plotting functions in order to draw the same plot different ways or arrange plots in a composite figure, depending upon the presentation.

MWE and Full message:

```python
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
import warnings

warnings.simplefilter("error", category=Warning)
plt.plot([1,2,3])
plt.legend()
```
(no error, plot appears)
> No artists with labels found to put in legend.  Note that artists whose label start with an underscore are ignored when legend() is called with no argument.

### Proposed solution

Make this a true `UserWarning`.  Currently, it's a `logging.LogRecord` of level=`logging.Warning`.  
```python
log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
...
log.warning(<this message>)
```
**Additional pro:** In the future, it would be easier to find and work on this warning if it is, in fact, a true `Warning`.

**Con:** It won't be handled by the logging infrastructure.  But legend.py uses the default module logger, and the only `LogRecord` that it emits is this one.  The only situation I could think of where this might matter is if a user has some override of global logging setup (by calling `logging.setLoggerClass()`) but does not apply a similar philosophy to capturing warnings in STDERR.

Also add test_legend_nolabels_warning()

## PR summary
After discussion in the linked issue, it was determined that `warnings.warn()` was better than `log.warning()` for alerting users that we can't draw a legend if there are no labels.  This PR implements the change from `log.warning()` to `warnings.warn` (actually, `_api.warn_external()`)

#27145

Also add test_legend_nolabels_warning()

## PR summary
After discussion in the linked issue, it was determined that `warnings.warn()` was better than `log.warning()` for alerting users that we can't draw a legend if there are no labels.  This PR implements the change from `log.warning()` to `warnings.warn` (actually, `_api.warn_external()`)

#27145

Also add test_legend_nolabels_warning()

## PR summary
After discussion in the linked issue, it was determined that `warnings.warn()` was better than `log.warning()` for alerting users that we can't draw a legend if there are no labels.  This PR implements the change from `log.warning()` to `warnings.warn` (actually, `_api.warn_external()`)

#27145

Also add test_legend_nolabels_warning()

## PR summary
After discussion in the linked issue, it was determined that `warnings.warn()` was better than `log.warning()` for alerting users that we can't draw a legend if there are no labels.  This PR implements the change from `log.warning()` to `warnings.warn` (actually, `_api.warn_external()`)

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-09-03T00:02:29Z","updated_at":"2023-09-03T00:02:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

Not sure if this should go in "bug" or "doc", but treating error message as a doc issue.

The error message for boolean fancy indexing with the wrong shape refers to "dimension" for two different concepts: the axis number, and the length of that axis.  By itself it's fine, but can be mildly confusing appears deep in some code where I'm not sure the dimensions of the array that's being indexed.  e.g.:

```python
import numpy as np
arr = np.zeros((3,2))
mask = np.array([True, True])
arr[mask]
```
```
IndexError: boolean index did not match indexed array along dimension 1; 
dimension is 2 but corresponding boolean dimension is 1
```

### Idea or request for content:

Perhaps change it to 
```
IndexError: boolean index did not match indexed array along axis 1; 
dimension is 2 but corresponding boolean dimension is 1
```

Maybe it's a bit of bikeshedding, but it seems clearer to me to use "axis" instead of "dimension" for the first case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24624/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24624/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Sorry if this already exists, but I didn't see it.  Most if not all of the sparse [functions](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/sparse.html) still return sparse matrices.   I have higher level algorithm code that uses matrix-vector products and helper functions that create sparse arrays and matrices.  It would be nice for the higher level code if I could restrict the helper function return values to be sparse arrays.  I currently can't do that because the helper functions use things like `sparse.block_diag()` and `sparse.eye()`.  The consequence for higher level code is that it needs to cast all `todense()` calls inside `np.asarray()`.

Example:
```python
x: np.ndarray
G: sparse.spmatrix
Qinv: sparse.spmatrix
updates: np.ndarray = G @ x
root_Qinv: np.matrix = np.linalg.cholesky(Qinv.todense())
standardized_updates: np.matrix = root_Qinv @ updates

stats.kstest(standardized_updates.flatten(), stats.norm.cdf)
# error because flatten() doesn't work on numpy matrix
```

### Describe the solution you'd like.

```python
def to_sparray(sparse_thing):
    if isinstance(sparse_matrix, scipy.sparse.sparray):
        # Probably needs to be idemptotent for forwards compatibility
        return sparse_thing
   elif isinstance(sparse_thing, scipy.sparse.csr_matrix):
        # convert to csr_array
   # all other cases
```

This would allow me to push all the array-vs-matrix code into the lower level functions, rather than handling among the linear algebra whenever I need a dense array.
### Describe alternatives you've considered.

A) Add a kwarg to allow all sparse matrix creation functions to return a sparse array instead, if possible.  However, this would probably be implemented in a decorator that calls the proposed function.

B) change `spmatrix.todense()` to return an array, not a matrix.  Not sure what backwards compatibility issues this creates, however.

C) Do nothing here.  In user code, continue to use sparse matrices and wrap `np.asarray(sparse_mat.todense())`

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

I read through https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/8162, but didn't see anything about Please describe.

Sorry if this already exists, but I didn't see it.  The new sparray classes are promising, but not yet helpful in my code.  Most if not all of the sparse [functions](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/sparse.html) still return sparse matrices.  This includes stacking functions, which I use to create the update and inverse covariance matrices for Kalman Smoothing, which I call `G` and `Qinv`.

In the calling code, I then use these for matrix-vector products (duh) as well as in numpy.linalg decompositions, which I then use in matrix-vector products.  The problem is that the results are sometimes numpy arrays, which work with `flatten()` and `reshape()`, sometimes numpy matrices, which don't.  E.g.
```python
x: np.ndarray
G: sparse.spmatrix
Qinv: sparse.spmatrix
updates: np.ndarray = G @ x
standardized_updates: np.matrix = np.linalg.cholesky(Qinv.todense()) @ updates

stats.kstest(standardized_updates.flatten(), stats.norm.cdf)
# error because flatten() doesn't work on numpy matrix
```
If I could cast the matrices `G` and `Qinv` to sparse arrays _in the function that creates them_, I wouldn't have to worry about matrices vs arrays in the higher-level calling code.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

```python
def to_sparray(sparse_thing):
    if isinstance(sparse_matrix, scipy.sparse.sparray):
        # Probably needs to be idemptotent for forwards compatibility
        return sparse_thing
   elif isinstance(sparse_thing, scipy.sparse.csr_matrix):
        # convert to csr_array
   # all other cases
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

A) Add a kwarg to allow all sparse matrix creation functions to return a sparse array instead.  However, this would probably be implemented in a decorator that calls the proposed function.

B) change `spmatrix.todense()` to return an array, not a matrix.  Not sure what backwards compatibility issues this creates, however.

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Sorry if this already exists, but I didn't see it.  Most if not all of the sparse [functions](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/sparse.html) still return sparse matrices.   I have higher level algorithm code that uses matrix-vector products and helper functions that create sparse arrays and matrices.  It would be nice for the higher level code if I could restrict the helper function return values to be sparse arrays.  I currently can't do that because the helper functions use things like `sparse.block_diag()` and `sparse.eye()`.  The consequence for higher level code is that it needs to cast all `todense()` calls inside `np.asarray()`.

Example:
```python
x: np.ndarray
G: sparse.spmatrix
Qinv: sparse.spmatrix
updates: np.ndarray = G @ x
root_Qinv: np.matrix = np.linalg.cholesky(Qinv.todense())
standardized_updates: np.matrix = root_Qinv @ updates

stats.kstest(standardized_updates.flatten(), stats.norm.cdf)
# error because flatten() doesn't work on numpy matrix
```

### Describe the solution you'd like.

```python
def to_sparray(sparse_thing):
    if isinstance(sparse_matrix, scipy.sparse.sparray):
        # Probably needs to be idemptotent for forwards compatibility
        return sparse_thing
   elif isinstance(sparse_thing, scipy.sparse.csr_matrix):
        # convert to csr_array
   # all other cases
```

This would allow me to push all the array-vs-matrix code into the lower level functions, rather than handling among the linear algebra whenever I need a dense array.
### Describe alternatives you've considered.

A) Add a kwarg to allow all sparse matrix creation functions to return a sparse array instead, if possible.  However, this would probably be implemented in a decorator that calls the proposed function.

B) change `spmatrix.todense()` to return an array, not a matrix.  Not sure what backwards compatibility issues this creates, however.

C) Do nothing here.  In user code, continue to use sparse matrices and wrap `np.asarray(sparse_mat.todense())`

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

Describe your issue.

I have a colleague who is attempting to use `odeint` to integrate a differential equation. This produces a large volume (>100k lines) of warning messages that, whilst real, they would like to filter as they pollute the Jupyter notebooks and are emitted in the Terminal window, etc.
The same noise is emitted in `solve_ivp` if `method='LSODA'` is used.

We have tried redirecting stdout, stderr, filtering Python warnings, etc, to no success. The message is emitted from [lsoda.f](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/1ab82fffd30f3bf936c1118e55617a978f9b7e90/scipy/integrate/odepack/lsoda.f#L1339), which calls [xerrwv](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/1ab82fffd30f3bf936c1118e55617a978f9b7e90/scipy/integrate/odepack/xerrwv.f#L1).

From reading lsoda.f it should be [possible set the logical unit that the messages are printed to, or to suppress warning messages](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/scipy/integrate/odepack/lsoda.f#L798). The default is to print to logical unit 6, which I think is stdout, so why doesn't the redirection of stdout work?

My preference at the moment would be to sort out where the messages are directed to, followed by addition of a parameter to the module that would turn off the warning messages.

@llimeht

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import contextlib
import io
import numpy
import numpy as np
from scipy.integrate import odeint


def dxdt(x, t):
    return [ x[0]**3 - 4. * x[1],
             x[0] - x[1] ]


f = io.StringIO()
with contextlib.redirect_stdout(f), contextlib.redirect_stderr(f):
    t = numpy.linspace(0, 8, 1001)
    x0 = np.array([-1.38918542, -7.87846202])
    x = odeint(dxdt, x0, t)
```


### Error message

```shell
lsoda--  warning..internal t (=r1) and h (=r2) are
       such that in the machine, t + h = t on the next step  
       (h = step size). solver will continue anyway
      in above,  r1 =  0.1750743642151D+00   r2 =  0.1257107638210D-16
<repeats ad nauseam>
```


### SciPy/NumPy/Python version information

in nbclient repo because  `nbconvert.preprocessors.ExecutePreprocessor` gets it's `execute_cell` method from `nbclient.client.NotebookClient`, so I hope this is the right place.

I've been trying to run certain experiments in a Jupyter Notebook in order to take advantage of the nice layout of code and plots.  However, in order to do so repeatably and store other metadata, I set up and run the notebook in memory and convert to HTML.

When I want to use the results of the experiment in some of the calling code, I currently print it in a `nbformat.notebooknode.NotebookNode` cell and then read a notebook cell's ["outputs"] value.  But for objects whose `repr` isn't enough to reconstruct the object, is there a way to inspect the global scope of the `NotebookNode`?

MWE:
```python
import nbformat
from nbconvert.preprocessors import ExecutePreprocessor

nb = nbformat.v4.new_notebook()
nb["cells"] = [nbformat.v4.new_code_cell(
    source="foo=1\n"
    "print(foo)"
)]
ep = ExecutePreprocessor(timeout=-1)
ep.preprocess(nb)
```
Currently, I run 
```python
result_string = nb["cells"][0]["outputs"][0]["text"][:-1]
```

Desired 
```python
result = ep.<some function>("foo")
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbclient/issues/277/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbclient/issues/277/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbclient/issues/comments/1467180573","html_url":"https://github.com/jupyter/nbclient/issues/277#issuecomment-1467180573","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/nbclient/issues/277","id":1467180573,"node_id":"IC_kwDODe1Ehc5Xc2Yd","user":{"login":"Jacob-Stevens-Haas","id":37048747,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MDQ4NzQ3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37048747?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","html_url":"https://github.com/Jacob-Stevens-Haas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Jacob-Stevens-Haas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-03-14T00:52:49Z","updated_at":"2023-03-14T00:52:49Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Sure, that's an option. My experiment runner creates a notebook with the appropriate code and parameters, then runs `nbconvert.ExecutePreprocessor(kernel=temp_kernel).preprocess(nb)`, then saves it as an HTML file.

Describe the workflow you want to enable

I have a subclass of `numpy.ndarray` which keeps track of axis labels: which axes represent time, spatial directions, independent samples, and response variables.  I have a `transform()` step in my pipeline that runs
`x = check_array(x, accept_sparse=("csr", "csc"), ...)`.
It's nice that this allows me to handle both numpy arrays and sparse inputs .  However, this [line](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/b73ec3c3b39f2fcda37f943f47cf90b8f46253c4/sklearn/utils/validation.py#L847) of `scipy.utils.validation.check_array` downcasts anything that isn't a sparse array to a numpy array.  This is because `numpy.asarray` does not allow interception via `__array_ufunc__` or `__array_function__`

### Describe your proposed solution

`isinstance` check to see if `array` is already a numpy array.  Not sure exactly how to enforce `order` and `dtype`, however.

### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant

User code instead repeats validation code from check_array.  In my case, that means importing the private function `_ensure_sparse_format()` and skipping or copypasting the other validation checks.

### Additional context

Describe the issue linked to the documentation

Not sure if the `set_params()` documentation is accurate.  [Relevant section of Estimator API documentation](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/developers/develop.html#get-params-and-set-params)

> The `set_params` on the other hand *takes as input a dict* of the form `'parameter': value` and sets the parameter of the estimator using this dict [emphasis added]

As far as I can tell, the subtle issue is that `set_params` takes keyword arguments, and **not a `dict`**.  It only receives a dict if it has splatted kwargs (`**kwargs`)

The wording led me to believe that the following method signature was acceptable:
```python
    def set_params(self, params: dict):
```
It gives `TypeError: set_params() got an unexpected keyword argument '<param name>'`

### Suggest a potential alternative/fix

> The `set_params` on the other hand takes as input each of the arguments of `__init__`, splats them into a dict of the form `'parameter': value`, and sets the parameter of the estimator using this dict","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/23677/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/23677/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}
